(born December 20, 1957)[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) is an American bassist, vocalist and songwriter. He co-founded and played bass guitar for the rock bands [Minutemen](/wiki/Minutemen_(band) "Minutemen (band)") (1980–1985), [Dos](/wiki/Dos_(band) "Dos (band)") (1985–present), and [Firehose](/wiki/Firehose_(band) "Firehose (band)") (1986–1994). He began a solo career with the 1995 album *[Ball-Hog or Tugboat?](/wiki/Ball-Hog_or_Tugboat%3F "Ball-Hog or Tugboat?")* and has since released three additional solo albums, most recently in 2010 with *[Hyphenated-man](/wiki/Hyphenated-man "Hyphenated-man")*. He is also the frontman for the supergroup [Big Walnuts Yonder](/wiki/Big_Walnuts_Yonder "Big Walnuts Yonder") (2008–present), a member of the [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock") group [Banyan](/wiki/Banyan_(band) "Banyan (band)") (1997–present) and is involved with several other musical projects. From 2003 until 2013, he was the bass guitarist for [The Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges").

Musical artist

**Michael David Watt** (born December 20, 1957)[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) is an American bassist, vocalist and songwriter. He co-founded and played bass guitar for the rock bands [Minutemen](/wiki/Minutemen_(band) "Minutemen (band)") (1980–1985), [Dos](/wiki/Dos_(band) "Dos (band)") (1985–present), and [Firehose](/wiki/Firehose_(band) "Firehose (band)") (1986–1994). He began a solo career with the 1995 album *[Ball-Hog or Tugboat?](/wiki/Ball-Hog_or_Tugboat%3F "Ball-Hog or Tugboat?")* and has since released three additional solo albums, most recently in 2010 with *[Hyphenated-man](/wiki/Hyphenated-man "Hyphenated-man")*. He is also the frontman for the supergroup [Big Walnuts Yonder](/wiki/Big_Walnuts_Yonder "Big Walnuts Yonder") (2008–present), a member of the [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock") group [Banyan](/wiki/Banyan_(band) "Banyan (band)") (1997–present) and is involved with several other musical projects. From 2003 until 2013, he was the bass guitarist for [The Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges").

Watt has been called "one of the greatest bassists on the planet".[[2]](#cite_note-2) *[CMJ New Music](/wiki/CMJ_New_Music "CMJ New Music")* called Watt a "seminal [post-punk](/wiki/Post-punk "Post-punk") bass player".[[3]](#cite_note-3) Readers of *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* voted Mike Watt one of the "40 Greatest Bassists of All Time"[[4]](#cite_note-4) and *[LA Weekly](/wiki/LA_Weekly "LA Weekly")* awarded him the number six spot in "The 20 Best Bassists of All Time".[[5]](#cite_note-5) In November 2008, Watt received the *Bass Player Magazine* lifetime achievement award, presented by [Flea](/wiki/Flea_(musician) "Flea (musician)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers") dedicated their best-selling album, 1991's *[Blood Sugar Sex Magik](/wiki/Blood_Sugar_Sex_Magik "Blood Sugar Sex Magik")*, to Watt.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

Watt was born in [Portsmouth, Virginia](/wiki/Portsmouth,_Virginia "Portsmouth, Virginia").[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) His father was a [machinist's mate](/wiki/Machinist%27s_mate "Machinist's mate") in the Navy[[8]](#cite_note-chonin-8) and when he was young, Watt's family moved to [San Pedro, California](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles,_California "San Pedro, Los Angeles, California"), where he became good friends with [D. Boon](/wiki/D._Boon "D. Boon").[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) Watt and Boon picked up bass and guitar, respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) Watt was a fan of [T. Rex](/wiki/T.Rex_(band) "T.Rex (band)")[[9]](#cite_note-calvert-9) and [Blue Öyster Cult](/wiki/Blue_%C3%96yster_Cult "Blue Öyster Cult"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) while Boon's exposure to rock music was limited to [Creedence Clearwater Revival](/wiki/Creedence_Clearwater_Revival "Creedence Clearwater Revival"), another Watt favorite.[[9]](#cite_note-calvert-9)

### The Minutemen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Minutemen")]

Main article: [Minutemen (band)](/wiki/Minutemen_(band) "Minutemen (band)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Mike_watt_1982.jpg/250px-Mike_watt_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mike_watt_1982.jpg)

Watt in 1982

In 1978, Watt and Boon formed a band called [The Reactionaries](/wiki/The_Reactionaries_(band) "The Reactionaries (band)") with drummer [George Hurley](/wiki/George_Hurley "George Hurley") and vocalist [Martin Tamburovich](/wiki/Martin_Tamburovich "Martin Tamburovich").[[1]](#cite_note-sutherland-1) The band later became [Minutemen](/wiki/Minutemen_(band) "Minutemen (band)") with another drummer named [Frank Tonche](/wiki/Frank_Tonche "Frank Tonche"), who only lasted two shows with the group;[[11]](#cite_note-gnerre-11) Hurley, who had been in the short-lived [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") group Hey Taxi! at the time Minutemen first formed, rejoined Watt and Boon.[[11]](#cite_note-gnerre-11) After signing with [SST Records](/wiki/SST_Records "SST Records") in 1980, Minutemen began touring constantly, releasing a number of albums along the way. Their music was based on the speed, brevity, and intensity of punk, but included elements of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk").

Born with [Osgood–Schlatter disease](/wiki/Osgood%E2%80%93Schlatter_disease "Osgood–Schlatter disease"),[[12]](#cite_note-ibarra106-12) Watt had surgeries on both knees in the early 1980s which limited touring in 1981.[[12]](#cite_note-ibarra106-12) Watt wrote all of the music for *[What Makes a Man Start Fires?](/wiki/What_Makes_a_Man_Start_Fires%3F "What Makes a Man Start Fires?")* as he was laid up after one of his knee surgeries, living with his mother at the time and needed to keep himself occupied.[[12]](#cite_note-ibarra106-12)

In 1984, Watt met [Black Flag](/wiki/Black_Flag_(band) "Black Flag (band)") bassist [Kira Roessler](/wiki/Kira_Roessler "Kira Roessler") during a Black Flag/Minutemen tour. They soon became romantically involved, and subsequently began collaborating on songs, including material on Minutemen's final album *[3-Way Tie (For Last)](/wiki/3-Way_Tie_(For_Last) "3-Way Tie (For Last)")*. They also formed a two-bass duo, [Dos](/wiki/Dos_(band) "Dos (band)"), and have since recorded and released three records.

Minutemen ended tragically on December 22, 1985, when Boon was killed in an automobile crash at the [age of 27](/wiki/27_Club "27 Club") while driving to [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") with his girlfriend. Their fifth full-length album, *3-Way Tie (For Last)*, had already been scheduled for release at the time of the accident. In the documentary film *[We Jam Econo](/wiki/We_Jam_Econo "We Jam Econo")*, Watt mentioned that the last time he saw Boon, he had received lyrics for 10 songs from critic and songwriter [Richard Meltzer](/wiki/Richard_Meltzer "Richard Meltzer") for a planned collaboration with Minutemen. Minutemen were also planning to record a triple album with the working title *3 Dudes, 6 Sides, 3 Studio, 3 Live* as way to counteract [bootleggers](/wiki/Bootleg_recording "Bootleg recording").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Firehose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Firehose")]

Main article: [Firehose (band)](/wiki/Firehose_(band) "Firehose (band)")

After Boon's death, Watt was profoundly [depressed](/wiki/Clinical_depression "Clinical depression"); he and Hurley initially intended to quit music altogether. [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth") invited Watt to hang out with them in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") in 1986; they recorded a cover of [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(entertainer) "Madonna (entertainer)")'s "Burnin' Up" (with additional guitars by Greg Ginn) on the first [Ciccone Youth](/wiki/Ciccone_Youth "Ciccone Youth") [EP](/wiki/EP_(format) "EP (format)"), and Watt played bass for two songs on the Sonic Youth album *[Evol](/wiki/Evol_(Sonic_Youth_album) "Evol (Sonic Youth album)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-adams-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Watt cites this period as critical in inspiring his post-Minutemen career saying, "The first thing I did was Thurston asked me to play bass on *Evol*. That was a big highlight, man. Like, 'What, you want me to play without D. Boon?'"[[16]](#cite_note-Mike_Watt_Interview-16)

Subsequently, [Ed Crawford](/wiki/Ed_Crawford "Ed Crawford"), a Minutemen fan who drove to [San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro,_Los_Angeles,_California "San Pedro, Los Angeles, California") from [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), persuaded the Watt/Hurley [rhythm section](/wiki/Rhythm_section "Rhythm section") to continue playing music.[[14]](#cite_note-adams-14) Firehose was formed soon after. Following three releases on [SST](/wiki/SST_Records "SST Records"), Firehose was signed to [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") by A&R man Jim Dunbar. Shortly after the release of 1993's *Mr. Machinery Operator*, the band decided to call it quits.

Watt and Kira married in 1987, but their marriage ended not long after Firehose's break-up. However, both their friendship and Dos have remained intact; they even recorded their third album, *[Justamente Tres](/wiki/Justamente_Tres "Justamente Tres")*, not long after their [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce").

### Solo career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Solo career")]

After working with Firehose, Watt began a solo career. His first album, *[Ball-Hog or Tugboat?](/wiki/Ball-Hog_or_Tugboat%3F "Ball-Hog or Tugboat?")*, featured appearances from dozens of musicians (many were Watt's peers from the 1980s SST era), including [Henry Rollins](/wiki/Henry_Rollins "Henry Rollins"), [Eddie Vedder](/wiki/Eddie_Vedder "Eddie Vedder"), [J Mascis](/wiki/J_Mascis "J Mascis"), [Carla Bozulich](/wiki/Carla_Bozulich "Carla Bozulich"), [Evan Dando](/wiki/Evan_Dando "Evan Dando"), members of [Sonic Youth](/wiki/Sonic_Youth "Sonic Youth"), [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers"), [Frank Black](/wiki/Frank_Black "Frank Black"), [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Soul Asylum](/wiki/Soul_Asylum "Soul Asylum"), [Jane's Addiction](/wiki/Jane%27s_Addiction "Jane's Addiction"), the [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") and the [Screaming Trees](/wiki/Screaming_Trees "Screaming Trees"). The album and its supporting tour were Watt's first taste of mainstream fame, when Vedder and [Dave Grohl](/wiki/Dave_Grohl "Dave Grohl") of [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)") were part of his touring group. After Vedder returned to his [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam") commitments and Grohl began working with his new band [Foo Fighters](/wiki/Foo_Fighters "Foo Fighters"), Watt formed his only four-piece touring group to date, [The Crew Of The Flying Saucer](/wiki/The_Crew_Of_The_Flying_Saucer "The Crew Of The Flying Saucer"), featuring guitarist [Nels Cline](/wiki/Nels_Cline "Nels Cline") and two drummers.

In 1996, Watt contributed bass guitar on two songs for [Porno for Pyros](/wiki/Porno_for_Pyros "Porno for Pyros")' second album, *[Good God's Urge](/wiki/Good_God%27s_Urge "Good God's Urge")*, filling in for [Martyn LeNoble](/wiki/Martyn_LeNoble "Martyn LeNoble") who quit the band during recording sessions. Watt subsequently ended up being the bassist for the band's tour that followed the release of the album.

He made an appearance in an episode of [Cartoon Network](/wiki/Cartoon_Network "Cartoon Network")'s *[Space Ghost Coast to Coast](/wiki/Space_Ghost_Coast_to_Coast "Space Ghost Coast to Coast")*.

In 1997, Watt released *[Contemplating the Engine Room](/wiki/Contemplating_the_Engine_Room "Contemplating the Engine Room")*, a punk rock [song cycle](/wiki/Song_cycle "Song cycle") using naval life as an extended [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") for both Watt's family history (the album has a picture of his father in his Navy uniform on the cover) and the Minutemen. The album, which was critically well received, features the trio of musicians [Nels Cline](/wiki/Nels_Cline "Nels Cline") on guitar, [Stephen Hodges](/wiki/Stephen_Hodges "Stephen Hodges") on drums, and Watt as the only singer.

Watt went on to play in such groups as [Banyan](/wiki/Banyan_(band) "Banyan (band)") (with [Stephen Perkins](/wiki/Stephen_Perkins "Stephen Perkins") and Nels Cline) and [Hellride](/wiki/Hellride "Hellride"), a sometime live outfit that plays cover versions of [Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges") songs. He also played in [Wylde Ratttz](/wiki/Wylde_Ratttz "Wylde Ratttz") with Sonic Youth's [Thurston Moore](/wiki/Thurston_Moore "Thurston Moore") and The Stooges' [Ron Asheton](/wiki/Ron_Asheton "Ron Asheton"), recording a song for the film *[Velvet Goldmine](/wiki/Velvet_Goldmine "Velvet Goldmine")*.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Watt also recorded a bass line to send to the [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") space-folk band The Clubber Lang Gang for their record *Now Here This*, on the track "For the Broken People". Starting in mid-2011, Watt began playing bass for a psychedelic/progressive rock band called [Anywhere](/wiki/Anywhere_(band) "Anywhere (band)") with the [Mars Volta](/wiki/Mars_Volta "Mars Volta")'s [Cedric Bixler-Zavala](/wiki/Cedric_Bixler-Zavala "Cedric Bixler-Zavala") and Christian Eric Beaulieu of [Triclops!](/wiki/Triclops_(band) "Triclops (band)") In 2015 Mike Watt joined the [Waywords and Meansigns](/wiki/Waywords_and_Meansigns "Waywords and Meansigns") project, a collaborative project setting James Joyce's [Finnegans Wake](/wiki/Finnegans_Wake "Finnegans Wake") to music.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Illness, recovery and The Stooges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Illness, recovery and The Stooges")]

[![Watt performing with Iggy and the Stooges in 2010](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Mike_Watt%2C_The_Stooges%2C_ATP_Festival%2C_May_2010.jpg/250px-Mike_Watt%2C_The_Stooges%2C_ATP_Festival%2C_May_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mike_Watt,_The_Stooges,_ATP_Festival,_May_2010.jpg)

Watt performing with Iggy and the Stooges at an [ATP Festival](/wiki/All_Tomorrow%27s_Parties_(festival) "All Tomorrow's Parties (festival)") in [Minehead](/wiki/Minehead "Minehead") in 2010.

In January 2000, Watt fell ill with an infection of his [perineum](/wiki/Perineum "Perineum"),[[20]](#cite_note-splendid-20) forcing him into emergency surgery and nine weeks of bedrest in his San Pedro apartment. Initially unable to play his bass, he rebuilt his strength with intense practice as well as live club gigs where he performed sets of Stooges covers with Hellride in [California](/wiki/California "California") and with [J Mascis](/wiki/J_Mascis "J Mascis") and [Dinosaur Jr.](/wiki/Dinosaur_Jr. "Dinosaur Jr.") drummer [Murph](/wiki/Murph_(drummer) "Murph (drummer)") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") under the name Hellride East.

In 2000, Mascis asked Watt to participate in a world tour behind Mascis' first post-Dinosaur Jr. release, [J Mascis and the Fog](/wiki/J_Mascis_and_the_Fog "J Mascis and the Fog")'s *More Light*. At several of the shows, [Ron Asheton](/wiki/Ron_Asheton "Ron Asheton") formerly of The Stooges joined Mascis and Watt onstage, wherein the group would play entire sets of Stooges songs. Watt and Mascis later joined Asheton and his brother, Stooges drummer [Scott Asheton](/wiki/Scott_Asheton "Scott Asheton"), for a one-time-only performance at a Belgian festival under the name **Asheton, Asheton, Mascis & Watt**. In 2001, Watt was one of several bassists invited to participate in the sessions for [Gov't Mule](/wiki/Gov%27t_Mule "Gov't Mule")'s *[The Deep End](/wiki/The_Deep_End,_Volume_1 "The Deep End, Volume 1")*, partly on the recommendation of [Primus](/wiki/Primus_(band) "Primus (band)")' [Les Claypool](/wiki/Les_Claypool "Les Claypool"). Watt and Gov't Mule recorded a cover version of [Creedence Clearwater Revival](/wiki/Creedence_Clearwater_Revival "Creedence Clearwater Revival")'s "Effigy" for the album. The sessions were immortalized in the documentary feature film *[Rising Low](/wiki/Rising_Low "Rising Low")*.

In 2002, Watt, along with [Pete Yorn](/wiki/Pete_Yorn "Pete Yorn") and members of [The Hives](/wiki/The_Hives "The Hives"), backed [Iggy Pop](/wiki/Iggy_Pop "Iggy Pop") for a short set of Stooges songs at that year's [Shortlist Music Prize](/wiki/Shortlist_Music_Prize "Shortlist Music Prize") ceremony, after which Watt was asked to play bass in the reunited Stooges lineup in 2003. The reunited Stooges played their first show in almost 20 years at the [Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Music_and_Arts_Festival "Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival") in May 2003.

### *The Secondman's Middle Stand*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The Secondman's Middle Stand")]

Main article: [The Secondman's Middle Stand](/wiki/The_Secondman%27s_Middle_Stand "The Secondman's Middle Stand")

Watt's third solo album *[The Secondman's Middle Stand](/wiki/The_Secondman%27s_Middle_Stand "The Secondman's Middle Stand")*, inspired by both his 2000 illness and one of his favorite books, [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante")'s *[The Divine Comedy](/wiki/The_Divine_Comedy "The Divine Comedy")*,[[21]](#cite_note-21) was released in 2004; one reviewer writes that the album is a "harrowing, funny, and genuinely moving stuff from a true American original".[[22]](#cite_note-22) For the first time since the Minutemen, Watt recorded the album with an "all-Pedro band", [Mike Watt and the Secondmen](/wiki/Mike_Watt_and_the_Secondmen "Mike Watt and the Secondmen"), consisting of organist Pete Mazich and drummer Jerry Trebotic, along with former [that dog.](/wiki/That_dog. "That dog.") vocalist [Petra Haden](/wiki/Petra_Haden "Petra Haden").

While promoting and touring behind *The Secondman's Middle Stand*, Watt announced plans for future recordings, stating that he intended to record as frequently as he did in the Minutemen days for as long as he could.[[23]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-23)

Watt would part amicably with Columbia/[Sony BMG](/wiki/Sony_BMG "Sony BMG") in 2005, after 14 years as both a solo artist and as one-third of Firehose.

### Unknown Instructors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unknown Instructors")]

Main article: [Unknown Instructors](/wiki/Unknown_Instructors "Unknown Instructors")

In 2005, another side project featuring Watt came to light with the announced September 20 release of *[The Way Things Work](/wiki/The_Way_Things_Work_(album) "The Way Things Work (album)")*, an album of [improvised music](/wiki/Musical_improvisation "Musical improvisation") under the group name, [Unknown Instructors](/wiki/Unknown_Instructors "Unknown Instructors") with George Hurley, [Saccharine Trust](/wiki/Saccharine_Trust "Saccharine Trust")'s Joe Baiza and [Jack Brewer](/wiki/Jack_Brewer_(musician) "Jack Brewer (musician)"), and [poet](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry")/[saxophonist](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone") Dan McGuire. A month after the album's release, the Unknown Instructors recorded a second album, *[The Master's Voice](/wiki/The_Master%27s_Voice "The Master's Voice")*, with [Pere Ubu](/wiki/Pere_Ubu_(band) "Pere Ubu (band)") frontman [David Thomas](/wiki/David_Thomas_(musician) "David Thomas (musician)") and artist [Raymond Pettibon](/wiki/Raymond_Pettibon "Raymond Pettibon") joining the core quartet of Watt, Hurley, McGuire and Baiza. A third album with the same lineup, *Funland*, was released in 2009 and features a semi-cover version (the original lyrics over new music) of [Captain Beefheart](/wiki/Captain_Beefheart "Captain Beefheart")'s "Frownland" (from the *[Trout Mask Replica](/wiki/Trout_Mask_Replica "Trout Mask Replica")* album). Basic tracks have already been laid down for the fourth Unknown Instructors album at Secondmen organist [Pete Mazich](/wiki/Pete_Mazich "Pete Mazich")'s Casa Hanzo studio in San Pedro.

Watt would further his interest in improvised music by forming a trio, Los Pumpkinheads, with former [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") keyboardist [Money Mark](/wiki/Money_Mark "Money Mark") and Caroline Bermudez.

On December 14, 2005, the McNally-Smith College of Music in [Saint Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota") announced the formation of the Mike Watt Bass Guitar Scholarship, which is to be awarded annually to a bass major starting in the Fall of 2006.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In March 2006, Watt took part in the performance at [Disney Hall](/wiki/Disney_Hall "Disney Hall"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), of [Glenn Branca](/wiki/Glenn_Branca "Glenn Branca")'s "Hallucination City" Symphony #13.

### *The Weirdness*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Weirdness")]

Main article: [The Weirdness](/wiki/The_Weirdness "The Weirdness")

In October 2006, Watt joined the rest of The Stooges at recording engineer [Steve Albini](/wiki/Steve_Albini "Steve Albini")'s [Electrical Audio](/wiki/Electrical_Audio "Electrical Audio") Studio in [Chicago, Illinois](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois") to record *[The Weirdness](/wiki/The_Weirdness "The Weirdness")*, the first Stooges studio album since 1973's *[Raw Power](/wiki/Raw_Power "Raw Power")*. The album was released on March 6, 2007, and much of Watt's 2007 was devoted to Stooges duties, including the band's first full-length U.S. tour since the band's reformation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Watt also worked on two other projects during this time period: Funanori, a musical collaboration with Kaori Tsuchida, guitarist of [The Go! Team](/wiki/The_Go!_Team "The Go! Team"), on [shamisen](/wiki/Shamisen "Shamisen") and other instruments, and Pelicanman (named after the closing track on *The Secondman's Middle Stand*) with Petra Haden.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The first three songs recorded by Watt and Tsuchida as Funanori were released on a split EP with [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") band LITE in the summer of 2007 by Transduction Records. Watt also contributed a cover of [Blue Öyster Cult](/wiki/Blue_%C3%96yster_Cult "Blue Öyster Cult")'s "Burning For You", recorded with Haden, Nels Cline, Money Mark Nishita, and [Red Hot Chili Peppers](/wiki/Red_Hot_Chili_Peppers "Red Hot Chili Peppers") drummer [Chad Smith](/wiki/Chad_Smith "Chad Smith"), to the all-star compilation album *[Guilt by Association](/wiki/Guilt_by_Association_Vol._1 "Guilt by Association Vol. 1")*, released in August by the independent label [Engine Room Recordings](/wiki/Engine_Room_Recordings "Engine Room Recordings").

On June 9, 2007, Watt was the live narrator for the [silent movie](/wiki/Silent_movie "Silent movie") *[Brand Upon the Brain!](/wiki/Brand_Upon_the_Brain! "Brand Upon the Brain!")* at the [Egyptian Theatre](/wiki/Egyptian_Theatre "Egyptian Theatre") in [Hollywood, California](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles,_California "Hollywood, Los Angeles, California").

### Big Walnuts Yonder

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Big Walnuts Yonder")]

Main article: [Big Walnuts Yonder](/wiki/Big_Walnuts_Yonder "Big Walnuts Yonder")

Big Walnuts Yonder is an American [supergroup](/wiki/Supergroup_(music) "Supergroup (music)") formed in 2008 consisting of bassist/vocalist Watt, guitarist [Nels Cline](/wiki/Nels_Cline "Nels Cline") from [Wilco](/wiki/Wilco "Wilco"), drummer [Greg Saunier](/wiki/Greg_Saunier "Greg Saunier") from [Deerhoof](/wiki/Deerhoof "Deerhoof"), and guitarist/vocalist Nick Reinhart from [Tera Melos](/wiki/Tera_Melos "Tera Melos").[[27]](#cite_note-Pitchfork-27) They released their debut album in March 2017 through [Sargent House](/wiki/Sargent_House "Sargent House").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Clenchedwrench label, Floored by Four, and Firehose reunion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Clenchedwrench label, Floored by Four, and Firehose reunion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Mike_Watt_2013.jpg/250px-Mike_Watt_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mike_Watt_2013.jpg)

Mike Watt, 2013.

Watt reunited with the final Black Gang lineup of Nels Cline and Bob Lee to record a new album, tentatively titled *My Shibun No Hi*, in 2008. No release date has yet been set for the album.[[29]](#cite_note-shibun-29)

Continuing his exploration of both improvised music and the Japanese independent music scene, Watt, along with Nels Cline and producer Kramer, formed Brother's Sister's Daughter with mi-gu members Shimizu "Shimmy" Hirotaka and Yuko Araki to do a two-week tour of the country in late 2008 and record an album afterward. The album was mixed down by Hirotake but has not lined up a label or release date yet. Since then, two more albums by the band have been recorded but have yet

Watt and Cline teamed up in New York to form [Floored by Four](/wiki/Floored_by_Four "Floored by Four") with ex-Cibo Matto keyboardist [Yuka Honda](/wiki/Yuka_Honda "Yuka Honda") and [Lounge Lizards](/wiki/Lounge_Lizards "Lounge Lizards") drummer Dougie Bowne. The group debuted at Central Park Summerstage on August 1, and recorded their first self-titled album right afterward.[[30]](#cite_note-fbf_wsj-30) The album was released in late September 2010 on [Sean Lennon](/wiki/Sean_Lennon "Sean Lennon")'s Chimera Music label. Honda would later rejoin Watt and Cline for a second Brother's Sister's Daughter tour and album session in Japan
[[31]](#cite_note-verbicide-31) (Cline and Honda became romantically involved – and subsequently engaged to be married – during the course of these projects[[30]](#cite_note-fbf_wsj-30)).

Watt is interviewed in the 2009 documentary film Live House, about the underground music scene in Japan.

Watt is using his clenchedwrench label to put out some other long-awaited recording projects from the stockpile he has accumulated over the past few years, starting with the fourth Dos album (*Dos y Dos*, released July 16, 2011), followed by the Spielgusher project with [Richard Meltzer](/wiki/Richard_Meltzer "Richard Meltzer") and mi-gu's Shimizu "Shimmy" Hirotaka and Yuko Araki (*Spielgusher*, released January 17, 2012) and two Il Sogno del Marinaio albums. Watt chose to start clenchedwrench after returning from his Japanese *Hyphenated-man* tour because "I've got so many proj[ect]s in the pipeline... and I want no hiccups with getting them out, so up goes my own freak flag flying."[[32]](#cite_note-clenchedwrench-32)

Firehose's reunion was formally announced as part of the lineup of the 2012 [Coachella Festival](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Music_and_Arts_Festival "Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival"). The band underwent a short tour in April centered around both Coachella dates.

### Il Sogno del Marinaio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Il Sogno del Marinaio")]

Main article: [Il Sogno del Marinaio](/wiki/Il_Sogno_del_Marinaio "Il Sogno del Marinaio")

In late November 2009, Watt traveled to Italy to tour and record at the invitation of Italian guitarist Stefano Pilia. The project band, Il Sogno Del Marinaio,(the name is [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") for *The Sailor's Dream*)[[33]](#cite_note-sogno-33) did a short six-date tour, after which recording for an album (later entitled *La Busta Gialla*) took place.

The band is an [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music") trio consisting of Watt on vocals and bass guitar, Stefano Pilla on guitar and vocals, and [Andrea Belfi](/wiki/Andrea_Belfi "Andrea Belfi") on drums and vocals.[[33]](#cite_note-sogno-33) The band has released two studio albums on [clenchedwrench](/wiki/Clenchedwrench "Clenchedwrench"): *La busta gialla* (2013) and *Canto Secondo* (2014).[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### *Hyphenated-Man*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hyphenated-Man")]

Main article: [Hyphenated-man](/wiki/Hyphenated-man "Hyphenated-man")

Watt recorded his first post-Columbia solo album, *[Hyphenated-man](/wiki/Hyphenated-man "Hyphenated-man")*, in two sessions 13 months apart, with The Missingmen at Studio G, the New York studio of former [Pere Ubu](/wiki/Pere_Ubu "Pere Ubu") bassist [Tony Maimone](/wiki/Tony_Maimone "Tony Maimone"). *Hypnenated-man* consists of 30 short songs inspired by the paintings of [Hieronymus Bosch](/wiki/Hieronymus_Bosch "Hieronymus Bosch").[[23]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-23) The first session for the album took place during a planned break in Watt's Spring 2009 tour, during which, in a deliberate move, only the drum and guitar tracks were completed. Watt overdubbed his vocal and bass tracks with Maimone in June 2010. The album was released in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") by [Parabolic Records](https://web.archive.org/web/20130727000052/http://parabolic.jp/) on October 6, 2010, and Watt undertook his first tour of the country as a solo artist in support of it (before his work with Brother's Sister's Daughter, he previously toured there as a sideman with J Mascis and as one-fifth of The Stooges). A release of the album for the rest of the world took place on Watt's newly established clenchedwrench label on March 1, 2011, with an accompanying 51-date US/Canadian tour occurring between March 10 and April 30.[[35]](#cite_note-2011tour-35)

### CUZ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: CUZ")]

Main article: [CUZ (band)](/wiki/CUZ_(band) "CUZ (band)")

In 2006, Watt met Sam Dook of [The Go! Team](/wiki/The_Go!_Team "The Go! Team") at [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out").[[36]](#cite_note-pollard-36) The two continued emailing and decided to form a band. They recorded some sessions in 2008 while Watt was touring Europe and finished the album over email.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The album, entitled *Tamatebako*, was released in 2014 under the band name [CUZ](/wiki/CUZ_(band) "CUZ (band)")[[36]](#cite_note-pollard-36) and features guest vocals by [Charles Plymell](/wiki/Charles_Plymell "Charles Plymell"), [DJ Scotch Egg](/wiki/DJ_Scotch_Egg "DJ Scotch Egg"), and fellow Go! Team member [Kaori Tsuchida](/wiki/Kaori_Tsuchida "Kaori Tsuchida").[[36]](#cite_note-pollard-36) The album title references [the mysterious box of Japanese legend](/wiki/Tamatebako "Tamatebako").[[38]](#cite_note-38)
A tour in 2015 followed.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### The Hand to Man Band

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Hand to Man Band")]

Main article: [The Hand to Man Band](/wiki/The_Hand_to_Man_Band "The Hand to Man Band")

In 2010, [Thollem McDonas](/wiki/Thollem_McDonas "Thollem McDonas"), [John Dieterich](/wiki/Deerhoof "Deerhoof"), Tim Barnes, and Watt formed an experimental improvisational supergroup.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Their debut album, *You Are Always On Our Minds*, was released in 2012.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### The Island

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Island")]

Watt and S. Howe of the band Schooner formed an [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music") project called The Island[[42]](#cite_note-42) and released *Listening to the Warning* in 2011 and *Lamia* in 2018.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Hidden Rifles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Hidden Rifles")]

In 2014,[[44]](#cite_note-hiddenrifles-44) Watt joined with Mark Shippy ([U.S. Maple](/wiki/U.S._Maple "U.S. Maple")), Jim Sykes (Invisible Things), Matthew Wascovich ([Scarcity Of Tanks](/wiki/Scarcity_Of_Tanks "Scarcity Of Tanks")), and [Norman Westberg](/wiki/Norman_Westberg "Norman Westberg") ([Swans](/wiki/Swans_(band) "Swans (band)")) to form the [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock") project band Hidden Rifles.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Their debut album, *Across The Neighborhoods*, was released in October 2017.[[44]](#cite_note-hiddenrifles-44)

### Afternoon Freak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Afternoon Freak")]

Along with keyboardist Matt Mottel and percussionist Danny Frankel, Watt formed the trio Afternoon Freak and released the album *The Blind Strut* in 2018.[[46]](#cite_note-joyful-46) The album was recorded in one day on January 14, 2017, at [BIG EGO](/wiki/Chris_Schlarb "Chris Schlarb") studios.[[46]](#cite_note-joyful-46)

### Tone Scientists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Tone Scientists")]

In 2018, Watt joined with [Bucky Pope](/wiki/Tar_Babies "Tar Babies"), [John Herndon](/wiki/John_Herndon "John Herndon"), [Vince Meghrouni](/wiki/Bazooka_(band) "Bazooka (band)"), and [Pete Mazich](/wiki/Pete_Mazich "Pete Mazich") to form the project band Tone Scientists.[[47]](#cite_note-47) A 7-inch consisting of a Bucky Pope original entitled "Nuts" and a cover of [Sun Ra](/wiki/Sun_Ra "Sun Ra")'s "[Tiny Pyramids](/wiki/Angels_and_Demons_at_Play "Angels and Demons at Play")" was released on Record Store Day 2018[[48]](#cite_note-48) by [Org Music](/wiki/Org_Music "Org Music").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Jumpstarted Plowhards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Jumpstarted Plowhards")]

Main article: [Jumpstarted Plowhards](/wiki/Jumpstarted_Plowhards "Jumpstarted Plowhards")

In 2019, Watt joined [Todd Congelliere](/wiki/Todd_Congelliere "Todd Congelliere") to form Jumpstarted Plowhards.[[50]](#cite_note-plowhards-50) Their debut album, *Round One*, was recorded for release on October 4, 2019, with [Recess Records](/wiki/Recess_Records "Recess Records").[[50]](#cite_note-plowhards-50) Drums are handled by a rotating roster including former Minutemen and fIREHOSE bandmate George Hurley, [Hole](/wiki/Hole_(band) "Hole (band)") drummer [Patty Schemel](/wiki/Patty_Schemel "Patty Schemel"), [The Secondmen](/wiki/The_Secondmen "The Secondmen")'s [Jerry Trebotic](/wiki/Jerry_Trebotic "Jerry Trebotic") and [The Missingmen](/wiki/The_Missingmen "The Missingmen")'s [Raul Morales](/wiki/Raul_Morales "Raul Morales").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The band plans to record five LPs before playing live.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Fitted

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fitted")]

Main article: [Fitted](/wiki/Fitted "Fitted")

Initially formed as a one-off group for [Wire](/wiki/Wire_(band) "Wire (band)")'s Drill festival in Los Angeles,[[53]](#cite_note-pearis-53)[[54]](#cite_note-helman-54) Watt, [Graham Lewis](/wiki/Graham_Lewis "Graham Lewis") ([Wire](/wiki/Wire_(band) "Wire (band)")), [Matthew Simms](/wiki/Matthew_Simms "Matthew Simms") (Wire), and [Bob Lee](/w/index.php?title=Bob_Lee_(drummer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bob Lee (drummer) (page does not exist)") ([The Black Gang](/wiki/The_Black_Gang "The Black Gang")) formed Fitted and decided to stay together and record.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The debut album, *[First Fits](/wiki/First_Fits "First Fits")*, was released in November 2019.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### mssv

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: mssv")]

Main article: [mssv](/wiki/Mssv "Mssv")

Watt played bass with guitarist [Mike Baggetta](/wiki/Mike_Baggetta "Mike Baggetta") and drummer [Jim Keltner](/wiki/Jim_Keltner "Jim Keltner") for Baggetta's *[Wall of Flowers](/wiki/Wall_of_Flowers "Wall of Flowers")* album in March 2019.[[57]](#cite_note-maiuri-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Watt joined Baggetta for a ten date March tour with Stephen Hodges substituting for Keltner.[[57]](#cite_note-maiuri-57)

The resulting trio named themselves mssv and recorded a live album entitled *Live Flowers*.[[59]](#cite_note-59) A studio album entitled *Main Steam Stop Valve* followed in 2020.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Three-Layer Cake

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Three-Layer Cake")]

In 2021, Watt with percussionist [Mike Pride](/wiki/Mike_Pride_(musician) "Mike Pride (musician)") and guitarist Brandon Seabrook formed Three-Layer Cake[[61]](#cite_note-61) and released an album, *Stove Top*, on [RareNoiseRecords](/wiki/RareNoiseRecords "RareNoiseRecords").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Flipper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Flipper")]

Main article: [Flipper (band)](/wiki/Flipper_(band) "Flipper (band)")

Mike Watt was recruited by Flipper's Ted Falconi and Stephen DePace to play bass for a 2019 summer European tour with [David Yow](/wiki/David_Yow "David Yow") ([The Jesus Lizard](/wiki/The_Jesus_Lizard "The Jesus Lizard"), [Scratch Acid](/wiki/Scratch_Acid "Scratch Acid")) substituting for the retired Bruce Loose and the otherwise engaged then-current Flipper bassist, Rachel Theole. The shows were billed as **Flipper with Yow and Watt**.

In 2022, Flipper announced that they would tour again, with Watt returning on both bass and lead vocals. A European tour was announced, but cancelled due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") concerns. The band then quickly booked an autumn US tour with the same lineup.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Guest appearances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Iggy_Pop_%285%29.jpg/250px-Iggy_Pop_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iggy_Pop_(5).jpg)

Mike Watt and Iggy Pop at Sziget, 2006

In November 2006, Watt revealed to [Pitchfork Media](/wiki/Pitchfork_Media "Pitchfork Media") that he recorded for an album by singer [Kelly Clarkson](/wiki/Kelly_Clarkson "Kelly Clarkson"), a studio assignment that he took at the invitation of his "old friend", producer/engineer [David Kahne](/wiki/David_Kahne "David Kahne").[[63]](#cite_note-63) Clarkson's *[My December](/wiki/My_December "My December")* album was released in 2007.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Watt and drummer [Stephen Hodges](/wiki/Stephen_Hodges "Stephen Hodges") joined violinist [Chris Murphy](/wiki/Chris_Murphy_(violinist) "Chris Murphy (violinist)") for "Blues for Bukowski" on his Murphy's album *Luminous*.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Hodges had previously worked with Watt on *Contemplating the Engine Room*.[[57]](#cite_note-maiuri-57)

Watt appeared on the track "Moon Burnt Mountain" on *Anywhere II* by [Anywhere](/wiki/Anywhere_(band) "Anywhere (band)").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Watt played bass on two tracks for the [Black Flag](/wiki/Black_Flag_(band) "Black Flag (band)") tribute album *[Gimmie Gimmie Gimmie: Reinterpreting Black Flag](/wiki/Gimmie_Gimmie_Gimmie:_Reinterpreting_Black_Flag "Gimmie Gimmie Gimmie: Reinterpreting Black Flag")* alongside [Dez Cadena](/wiki/Dez_Cadena "Dez Cadena") and [Keith Morris](/wiki/Keith_Morris "Keith Morris").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 2018, [Black Moth Super Rainbow](/wiki/Black_Moth_Super_Rainbow "Black Moth Super Rainbow") released a [7-inch](/wiki/7-inch "7-inch") for [Record Store Day](/wiki/Record_Store_Day "Record Store Day") with Watt and [Flea](/wiki/Flea_(musician) "Flea (musician)") covering "[Drippy Eye](/wiki/Dandelion_Gum "Dandelion Gum")".[[69]](#cite_note-69) Watt had earlier appeared on the song "Black Yogurt" on Black Moth Super Rainbow's *Drippers* EP in 2008.[[70]](#cite_note-colly-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) The same year Watt appeared on Macedonia's Bernays Propaganda song "Ništo Nema da ne Razdeli" for the *Songs From Under the Floorboard, Vol. 1* compilation album put out by [DJ Dave Cantrell](/wiki/KXRY "KXRY").[[72]](#cite_note-floorboard-72) The album is a charitable compilation with proceeds going to [Planned Parenthood](/wiki/Planned_Parenthood "Planned Parenthood").[[72]](#cite_note-floorboard-72)

Watt also appeared on a split 7-inch with MB Jones playing on the song "Jail for Trees" in April 2018.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In October 2018, Watt joined [Abby Travis](/wiki/Abby_Travis "Abby Travis"), [Keith Morris](/wiki/Keith_Morris "Keith Morris"), [Flea](/wiki/Flea_(musician) "Flea (musician)"), [Jennifer Finch](/wiki/Jennifer_Finch "Jennifer Finch"), [Chip Kinman](/wiki/The_Dils "The Dils") and others for a spoken word engagement at the [Roxy Theatre](/wiki/Roxy_Theatre_(West_Hollywood) "Roxy Theatre (West Hollywood)") entitled *War Stories: Tales of 70's & 80's Punk Mayhem Told By The Perpetrators Themselves*.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In 2019, Watt appeared with Macedonian jazz guitarist [Toni Kitanovski](/wiki/Toni_Kitanovski "Toni Kitanovski") on the track "Nisto nema da ne' razdeli" for [Bernays Propaganda](/wiki/Bernays_Propaganda "Bernays Propaganda")'s 2019 release, *Vtora mladost, treta svetska vojna* (*2nd Youth, 3rd World War*)[[75]](#cite_note-foster-75) The track was also donated to *Songs From Under the Floorboard, Vol. 1*, proceeds of which go to [Planned Parenthood](/wiki/Planned_Parenthood "Planned Parenthood").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

In 2025, Watt joined post-punk band, Break to Broken for a guest appearance playing bass on the track "No Care". The track was part of a 3 song 7" record called "No Care + 2" released via UK label, Engineer Records on July 15th 2025.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Watt has said he generally charges no fee to play on somebody's album saying "All payment is not in the coin...Anytime you play, you're investing in the next time you play."[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Literary endeavors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Literary endeavors")]

In 2003, Watt's first book, *Spiels Of A Minuteman*, was released by the [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") book publisher [L'Oie De Cravan](/wiki/L%27Oie_de_Cravan "L'Oie de Cravan").[[79]](#cite_note-79) The book, printed in both [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), contains all of Watt's song [lyrics](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics") from the Minutemen era as well as the tour journal he wrote during the Minutemen's only [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") tour with [Black Flag](/wiki/Black_Flag_(band) "Black Flag (band)"),[[80]](#cite_note-80) essays by former SST co-owner [Joe Carducci](/wiki/Joe_Carducci "Joe Carducci"), Sonic Youth's [Thurston Moore](/wiki/Thurston_Moore "Thurston Moore"), and [Blue Öyster Cult](/wiki/Blue_%C3%96yster_Cult "Blue Öyster Cult") lyricist and longtime Watt hero [Richard Meltzer](/wiki/Richard_Meltzer "Richard Meltzer"), and illustrations by [Raymond Pettibon](/wiki/Raymond_Pettibon "Raymond Pettibon") that had been used in all of the Minutemen's album artwork.

Watt published his second book in 2012. Entitled *Mike Watt: On and Off Bass*, the book is a photo memoir[[81]](#cite_note-81) featuring photos taken by Watt coupled with excerpts from Watt's Stooges tour diaries mixed with [free verse poems](/wiki/Free_verse "Free verse").[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In 2016, Watt contributed a chapter to [John Doe's](/wiki/John_Doe_(musician) "John Doe (musician)") memoir *[Under The Big Black Sun: A Personal History of L.A. Punk](/wiki/Under_the_Big_Black_Sun_(book) "Under the Big Black Sun (book)")*.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### James Joyce

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: James Joyce")]

Watt has long been a fan of [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce") having first discovered *[Ulysses](/wiki/Ulysses_(novel) "Ulysses (novel)")* while playing with Minutemen on a European tour with [Black Flag](/wiki/Black_Flag_(band) "Black Flag (band)").[[84]](#cite_note-perfectsound-84) He credits reading Joyce with helping him process the loss of D. Boon[[85]](#cite_note-norecess-85) and he used it as inspiration when writing *[Contemplating the Engine Room](/wiki/Contemplating_the_Engine_Room "Contemplating the Engine Room")*.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Watt has said that Joyce's [stream-of-consciousness](/wiki/Stream-of-consciousness "Stream-of-consciousness") style directly influenced *[Double Nickels on the Dime](/wiki/Double_Nickels_on_the_Dime "Double Nickels on the Dime")*[[84]](#cite_note-perfectsound-84) with songs like "June 16th", which is the date of [Bloomsday](/wiki/Bloomsday "Bloomsday").[[87]](#cite_note-wired-87) Watt attended the 2004 Bloomsday celebration in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")[[85]](#cite_note-norecess-85) and claimed that it was the first time he went to a town without having to perform a gig.[[85]](#cite_note-norecess-85) Watt attended Bloomsday again in 2008 while touring with [The Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges").[[87]](#cite_note-wired-87)

In 2008, Watt was invited to contribute music to an adaptation of Joyce's *[Chamber Music](/wiki/Chamber_Music_(poetry_collection) "Chamber Music (poetry collection)")*[[88]](#cite_note-88) released by [Fire Records](/wiki/Fire_Records_(UK) "Fire Records (UK)")[[89]](#cite_note-89)

In 2017, Watt participated in an international project setting Joyce's *[Finnegans Wake](/wiki/Finnegans_Wake "Finnegans Wake")* to music entitled *[Waywords and Meansigns](/wiki/Waywords_and_Meansigns "Waywords and Meansigns")*[[90]](#cite_note-90) It was released on May 4, 2017, to coincide with the 76th anniversary of the book's first publication.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### *The Watt from Pedro Show*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: The Watt from Pedro Show")]

Main article: [The Watt from Pedro Show](/wiki/The_Watt_from_Pedro_Show "The Watt from Pedro Show")

When he is not on tour, Watt hosts a regular [internet radio](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio") show, *The Watt from Pedro Show*,[[92]](#cite_note-92) a continuation of a program Watt had first done on a [low-power FM](/wiki/Low-power_FM "Low-power FM") station in the late 1990s.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The program debuted on May 19, 2001, and became so popular with Watt's fans that the website's host, Sightworks, temporarily forced the show offline on weekdays until a sponsor or other solution could be found. On January 10, 2006, *The Watt from Pedro Show* became available as a [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast").

### Porno for Pyros

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Porno for Pyros")]

Watt reconnected with Porno for Pyros in 2023 for a tour that was postponed while they finished a new single "Little Me", and is currently on that rescheduled tour; 2024’s Horns, Thorns En Halos Farewell Tour, in support of that single and the back catalog.

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Watt&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Watt's first bass was a [Kay](/wiki/Kay_Musical_Instrument_Company "Kay Musical Instrument Company") he purchased for $100.[[94]](#cite_note-94) He played a Fender Precision Bass formerly owned by [Fear](/wiki/Fear_(band) "Fear (band)") bassist [Derf Scratch](/wiki/Derf_Scratch "Derf Scratch") on *What Makes A Man Start Fires?*[[95]](#cite_note-95)

For most of his fIREHOSE tenure, Watt played a 1956 [Fender Precision Bass](/wiki/Fender_Precision_Bass "Fender Precision Bass").[[96]](#cite_note-thudstaffs-96) From 1995 until 1999, Watt predominately played a [non-reverse](/wiki/Gibson_Thunderbird#Non-Reverse_Thunderbirds "Gibson Thunderbird") [Gibson Thunderbird](/wiki/Gibson_Thunderbird "Gibson Thunderbird") which he altered with a [Bartolini pre-amp](/w/index.php?title=Bartolini_Pickups_and_Electronics&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bartolini Pickups and Electronics (page does not exist)") and [Grover](/wiki/Grover_Musical_Products,_Inc. "Grover Musical Products, Inc.") [tuning machines](/wiki/Machine_head "Machine head").[[96]](#cite_note-thudstaffs-96)

Following his 2000 illness, Watt switched to short-scale basses for live work. Watt played a 1963 [Gibson EB-3](/wiki/Gibson_EB-3 "Gibson EB-3") until it was stolen while on tour with [The Stooges](/wiki/The_Stooges "The Stooges").[[97]](#cite_note-premier-97) After the theft, Watt was gifted with a 1969 Gibson EB-3 and a [Gibson EB-0](/wiki/Gibson_EB-0 "Gibson EB-0").[[96]](#cite_note-thudstaffs-96)

More recently, Watt plays a signature "Wattplower" bass he designed with [Reverend Musical Instruments](/wiki/Reverend_Musical_Instruments "Reverend Musical Instruments").[[97]](#cite_note-premier-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) In early 2020, it was announced that a "Mark II" iteration of the bass would be released.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)
